summary: Rabba bar Sheila resolves the tohoro question: it means the top of the altar itself, citing the verse 'like the very sky for purity.' Three follow-up questions about Rabbi Shimon ish HaMitzpah's blood-sprinkling choreography are each resolved: why olah before chatat (the tamid IS an olah, so it goes first), what about the southeast corner with no yesod (olah applications are skipped there), and the direction of approach (always right, for service movement). A brief logical puzzle about which direction the verse equation runs is resolved. The page concludes with the Chamber of the Lambs contradiction between Yoma and Middot, setting up 16a's extended debate about tractate authorship.

display.title: Tohoro resolved: 'purity' means the altar's bare top surface
display.oneLine: Rabba bar Sheila identifies the altar's 'tohoro' as its exposed top surface, proved from a verse comparing it to the clear sky.
display.whats: Rabba bar Sheila resolves the final question from 15a about 'tohoro': what does 'the purity of the altar' mean? He answers that it means the top surface of the altar itself, proving it from the verse 'like the very sky for purity' - comparing the altar's upper surface to the sky.
display.hint: The word 'tohoro' means purity, but here it refers to the altar's upper surface. The verse in Exodus comparing it to sky-like purity is the proof.

learning.learnerQuestion: The sprinklings from 15a were said to go on the altar's 'tohoro,' its purity. What part of the altar does that actually name?
learning.coreTension: 'Tohoro' (purity) is an abstract word, but the sugya needs it to identify a concrete physical location on the altar where blood is sprinkled.
learning.coreMove: Rabba bar Sheila grounds the abstract word in architecture by citing a verse that compares the altar's upper surface to the clear sky, fixing 'tohoro' as the altar's exposed, cleared top.
learning.learningBlocker: It is tempting to hear 'purity' and think of a specially sanctified zone rather than a plain physical surface. The verse proof shows the term is doing ordinary descriptive work: the top is 'pure' simply because it is the cleared, exposed face of the altar.

takeaway.text: An ambiguous term in a halakhic ruling can be fixed to a precise physical referent by citing a verse that uses the same term in an unambiguous descriptive sense elsewhere.

learning.ahaMoment: The word 'purity' (tohoro) is being used architecturally - the 'pure part' of the altar is its top, the part exposed and cleared of incense ash.
learning.memoryAnchor: Tohoro = the altar top. 'Like the very sky' = exposed, upward-facing surface.

argumentFlow.label: Rabba bar Sheila: tohoro = altar top
argumentFlow.speaker: Rabba bar Sheila
argumentFlow.text: Tohoro refers to the top surface of the altar itself - the surface that is cleared and exposed. Proof: 'like the very sky for purity' compares the altar's upper face to the clear sky.

concepts.def: The 'purity' or top surface of the altar - the exposed upper face onto which the seven sprinklings of the inner service are directed.

requiresUnderstanding: The seven sprinklings on the inner altar from 15a
R. Shimon ish HaMitzpah's blood service questions

misconceptions.misconception: 'Tohoro' names a special, separately sanctified section of the altar.
misconceptions.correction: It simply means the altar's top surface, the part exposed and cleared, not a distinct sanctified zone.

visualizableElements.desc: Diagram of inner altar (golden altar) with the top surface highlighted as 'tohoro' - the target of seven sprinklings

quizSeeds.question: What does 'tohoro shel mizbeach' mean and how does Rabba bar Sheila prove it?
quizSeeds.answer: It means the top surface of the altar. Proof: the verse compares it to 'the very sky for purity' - the sky is the exposed upper surface analogously.

sugyot.finalRuling: Established: tohoro = the altar's top surface (per Rabba bar Sheila).

glossary.en: Its purity - here meaning the top surface of the altar, compared to sky-like purity in Exodus 24:10.

display.title: Three loose ends in R. Shimon's blood choreography, each tied off
display.oneLine: Why olah comes before chatat, why a yesod-less corner still gets blood, and why the priest always turns right are each resolved with a precise technical answer.
display.whats: Three follow-up questions about R. Shimon ish HaMitzpah's blood-sprinkling choreography are each resolved. First: why does R. Shimon put the olah requirement before the chatat requirement when approaching the altar corners? Second: if the northeast corner has no foundation (yesod), why apply blood there first? Third: which way does the priest turn at the corners - always to the right?
display.hint: Each of the three questions has a clean answer: (1) olah is the more frequent sacrifice so it comes first; (2) the northeast corner has no yesod for the olah but does for the chatat above the red line; (3) 'all turns are to the right' applies only during service movements, not to theoretical spatial counting.

learning.learnerQuestion: R. Shimon's dual olah-and-chatat blood rite raises three practical snags: why olah goes first, how a corner with no yesod can still receive blood, and which way the priest moves. How are these resolved?
learning.coreTension: Each question exposes a place where the abstract dual-requirement ruling has to meet a concrete physical constraint of the altar (sequence, foundation structure, walking direction), and it is not obvious in advance that the ruling survives contact with those constraints.
learning.coreMove: Each question gets resolved by pointing to a specific, narrower fact that absorbs the difficulty: the tamid's primary identity as an olah explains the sequence, the height distinction (above versus below the red line) explains why a yesod-less corner can still take the chatat application, and the rule about rightward turns is shown to govern actual movement, not abstract corner order.
learning.learningBlocker: It is easy to expect one unifying explanation for all three difficulties. Instead each is resolved independently, by a different specific principle, showing that a complex ritual choreography can rest on several narrow technical facts rather than one grand rule.

takeaway.text: A ritual procedure built from two overlapping sacrificial categories can be fully specified, sequence, exceptions, and direction of movement, once each point of friction is matched to its own governing principle rather than forced under a single explanation.

learning.ahaMoment: Each question has a precise technical answer - the sugya is showing how halakhic principles interact when a single act must satisfy two different sacrificial categories.
learning.memoryAnchor: Three questions, three answers: (1) olah first because tamid is an olah, (2) no yesod only matters below the red line (olah level), (3) 'always right' is for movement not theory.

argumentFlow.label: Why olah before chatat in the corner sequence?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: R. Shimon applies blood in the olah manner before the chatat manner at each corner. Why does olah take priority?

argumentFlow.label: Tamid IS an olah - so olah comes first
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The tamid itself is an olah. The olah identity precedes the chatat identity in this sacrifice, so olah-applications come first.

sourceRefs.note: Resolution: tamid is inherently an olah

argumentFlow.label: The northeast corner has no yesod - how can olah blood go there?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Olah blood must be given where there is a yesod (foundation) below it. But Tractate Middot states the northeast corner has no yesod. How does R. Shimon apply blood there?

sourceRefs.note: Second question: northeast corner lacks yesod

argumentFlow.label: The priest starts at northeast despite no yesod, applies chatat (above red line) only
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The priest starts at the northeast corner (the first one reached when ascending the ramp from the south and turning right). Since there is no yesod, the olah application is skipped there - only the above-red-line chatat application is made. The priest then continues to the other corners.

concepts.def: The altar's foundation base - a protruding ledge below the main altar surface. Olah blood must be applied where there is a yesod below the application point. The northeast corner lacks this ledge.

requiresUnderstanding: Olah blood applications require a yesod below
Chatat blood is placed above the red line (chut ha-sikra)
The Middot tractate description of the altar and its corners

misconceptions.misconception: R. Shimon applies olah-style blood at all four altar corners equally.
misconceptions.correction: The northeast corner, lacking a yesod, receives only the chatat application; the olah application is skipped there.

visualizableElements.desc: Altar diagram showing four corners with yesod indicated at three corners but absent from the northeast; the priest's path highlighted going right from the south ramp
Three-panel sequence: (1) olah first at corners with yesod; (2) northeast corner - chatat only; (3) 'always right' rule for movement direction

quizSeeds.question: Why does R. Shimon ish HaMitzpah apply olah-style blood before chatat-style blood at each altar corner?
quizSeeds.answer: Because the tamid is inherently an olah - its primary identity is as a burnt offering, so the olah application has priority.

sourceRefs.note: Resolution: tamid is inherently an olah

quizSeeds.question: How is the northeast corner problem resolved in R. Shimon's blood rite?
quizSeeds.answer: Since the northeast corner has no yesod (foundation), the olah application (which requires a yesod below) is omitted there. Only the chatat application above the red line is made at that corner.

sugyot.finalRuling: Resolved: all three questions about R. Shimon's corner sequence have clear halakhic answers.

glossary.en: Foundation ledge of the altar. Olah blood requires a yesod below the application point. The northeast corner lacks it, affecting R. Shimon's blood rite.

display.title: Which way does the equation run? Reading 'chatat upon olah'
display.oneLine: R. Shimon reads 'as a chatat upon the olah' as the olah identity triggering chatat-mode blood service, not as blood being physically poured onto the olah.
display.whats: A brief logical question: does the verse 'as a chatat upon the olah' mean the tamid gets chatat rules applied to it because it IS an olah - or does it mean the chatat blood is applied TO the olah? The direction of the equation matters for understanding R. Shimon's ruling.
display.hint: The verse could be read as 'treat it like a chatat because it is an olah' (the chatat mode derives from the olah identity) or 'apply chatat-blood onto the olah animal' (the chatat blood is poured on top of the olah). R. Shimon reads it as the former.

learning.learnerQuestion: The verse links 'chatat' and 'olah' in one phrase about the tamid. Does that mean apply chatat rules because it is an olah, or pour chatat blood onto the olah animal?
learning.coreTension: A single verse phrase can be read in two directions, with the olah identity governing the chatat rule or the chatat blood physically governing the olah animal, and each direction implies a different practical service.
learning.coreMove: R. Shimon fixes the direction: the tamid's status as an olah is what triggers the additional chatat-mode blood service, not a physical pouring of chatat blood onto an olah animal.
learning.learningBlocker: It is easy to read 'chatat upon olah' as a literal physical instruction, blood placed on top of an animal. R. Shimon's reading treats it instead as a conceptual trigger: one sacrificial identity activating a second mode of blood application.

takeaway.text: When a verse phrase joins two categories, the direction in which one term governs the other is itself a substantive interpretive choice, and R. Shimon resolves it here by reading the olah identity as triggering the chatat-mode requirement, not as a physical instruction.

learning.ahaMoment: The direction of the equation in a verse determines a completely different halakhic outcome - this is a key principle of derash in Talmudic reasoning.
learning.memoryAnchor: Which direction does the equation run? 'Chatat on olah' = chatat rules triggered by olah identity (R. Shimon) - not blood poured onto the animal.

argumentFlow.label: Which direction does 'chatat upon olah' run?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The verse says 'as a chatat upon the olah of the tamid.' Does this mean: apply chatat-rules to the tamid because it is an olah? Or: pour chatat blood physically onto the olah?

sourceRefs.note: Directional question about the verse equation

concepts.def: When a verse seems to contain two directives simultaneously. Here: chatat rules and olah identity in the same phrase. The interpretation depends on which element governs the other.

requiresUnderstanding: The mechanics of chatat and olah blood applications

misconceptions.misconception: 'Chatat upon olah' means chatat blood is physically poured onto the olah animal.
misconceptions.correction: The Gemara clarifies the phrase refers to the application mode being triggered, not a physical pouring of one blood onto another.

visualizableElements.desc: Two reading directions of the verse: arrow from 'chatat' to 'olah' (chatat rules govern because of olah identity) vs arrow from 'olah' to 'chatat' (blood poured onto olah animal)

quizSeeds.question: What is the ambiguity in the verse 'as a chatat upon the olah of the tamid'?
quizSeeds.answer: The verse could mean: (A) apply chatat-style rules to this sacrifice because it is also an olah, or (B) physically pour chatat blood onto the olah animal. R. Shimon reads (A).

sourceRefs.note: Directional question about the verse equation

sugyot.finalRuling: R. Shimon reads the verse as: the tamid's olah-identity triggers chatat-mode blood applications.

display.title: Same Temple, two floor plans: the Chamber of the Lambs contradiction opens 16a
display.oneLine: Tractate Tamid and Tractate Middot place the Chamber of the Lambs in different corners of the Beit HaMoked, setting up the next daf's authorship debate.
display.whats: The Gemara discusses a mishna in Tamid about the Chamber of the Lambs (Lishkat HaTelayim) - the room where the daily tamid lambs were kept. The Yoma description and the Tamid/Middot descriptions place this chamber in different corners of the Beit HaMoked complex. The contradiction sets up the next daf's extended debate about which tractate is authoritative.
display.hint: Tractate Yoma says the Chamber of the Lambs was in one corner; Tractate Middot says it was in a different corner. The Gemara needs to explain this apparent contradiction.

learning.learnerQuestion: Tamid and Middot both describe the four-chamber Beit HaMoked, but they put the Chamber of the Lambs in different corners. How can two descriptions of the same physical room disagree?
learning.coreTension: Tractate Yoma's mishna locates the Chamber of the Lambs in one corner of the Beit HaMoked, while Tractate Middot's description places it in a different corner, a direct topographical contradiction about a single physical structure.
learning.coreMove: Rather than resolving the contradiction here, the Gemara reaches for the same tool used on 14b: identify who authored the conflicting tractate, since Rav Huna's proposal that R. Eliezer ben Yaakov authored Middot opens the path to explaining the discrepancy through differing authorial perspectives.
learning.learningBlocker: It is easy to expect this sugya to resolve the contradiction outright. Instead it deliberately leaves the question open, setting up 16a's extended authorship debate as the actual resolution mechanism, the same method already used for the Tamid/Yoma incense-and-lamps contradiction.

takeaway.text: The Chamber of the Lambs is placed in different corners by Tamid and Middot, and this sugya raises the contradiction and proposes an authorship-based approach to it without resolving which location is correct, deferring the resolution to the next daf.

learning.ahaMoment: The physical layout of the Temple creates contradictions between tractates - resolving them requires identifying who authored each tractate and what their perspective was.
learning.memoryAnchor: Chamber of the Lambs: two locations, two tractates, one Temple. The contradiction launches the 16a debate.

argumentFlow.label: Tamid/Yoma: Chamber of Lambs in one location
argumentFlow.speaker: Mishna
argumentFlow.text: Tractate Yoma's Mishnah places the Chamber of the Lambs in one specific corner of the Beit HaMoked.

argumentFlow.label: Middot places it in a different corner
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Tractate Middot's description of the four Beit HaMoked chambers places the Chamber of the Lambs in a different corner, creating a direct contradiction.

argumentFlow.label: Who authored Middot?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Rav Huna proposes: Tractate Middot was authored by R. Eliezer ben Yaakov. If we know his position, we can understand the discrepancy.

sourceRefs.note: Rav Huna's authorship claim sets up 16a

concepts.def: Chamber of the Lambs - the room in the Beit HaMoked complex where the daily tamid lambs were kept, inspected, and prepared. Its exact location is disputed between Yoma and Middot.

requiresUnderstanding: The four-chamber structure of the Beit HaMoked
The relationship between Tractates Yoma, Tamid, and Middot

misconceptions.misconception: Tractates always agree on Temple topography since they describe the same physical structure.
misconceptions.correction: Different tractates may reflect different authorities with different spatial perspectives, producing genuine contradictions like the Chamber of the Lambs location.

visualizableElements.desc: Beit HaMoked floor plan with four chambers labeled; the Chamber of the Lambs shown in two different corners per the two contradictory tractate descriptions
Side-by-side: Yoma's location for the Chamber vs Middot's location

quizSeeds.question: What is the contradiction between Tractates Yoma and Middot regarding the Chamber of the Lambs?
quizSeeds.answer: Both tractates describe the four-chamber Beit HaMoked, but they place the Chamber of the Lambs in different corners. This contradiction drives the 16a debate about who authored Tractate Middot.

glossary.en: Chamber of the Lambs - where tamid lambs were kept before being offered. Its location is disputed between Yoma and Middot.
Its purity - here meaning the top surface of the altar, compared to sky-like purity in Exodus 24:10.
Foundation ledge of the altar. Olah blood requires a yesod below the application point. The northeast corner lacks it, affecting R. Shimon's blood rite.
Chamber of the Lambs - where tamid lambs were kept before being offered. Its location is disputed between Yoma and Middot.

rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: 'on the body of the altar itself' - what is the meaning of tohoro (purity)? It is the altar's cleared top - where incense was burned in the morning and cleared away, exposing the stone. 'Like the very sky for purity' - as clear as the sky.
Rashi: 'like the very sky for purity' proves it means the exposed upper face of the altar, like the clear open sky.
Rashi: 'olah requires a yesod' - the foundation ledge must be below the point where olah blood is applied.
Rashi: 'so that there is a yesod below it' - olah blood goes to the area where the foundation extends beneath.
Rashi: the Gemara quotes from Zevachim about where the olah blood must be placed relative to the yesod.
Rashi: 'for the olah at its base' - meaning the yesod must be present below the point of application.
Rashi: southeast corner - this corner had a yesod and was the first encountered when ascending the ramp and turning right.
Rashi: Tractate Middot (Chapter 3, Mishna 1) states: the yesod ran along the entire north face and entire west face.
Rashi: the yesod occupied one cubit at the east (where the north face ends) and one cubit at the south (where the west face ends).
Rashi: so the yesod ran: full north, full west, one cubit into east at the north end, and one cubit into south at the west end.
Rashi: therefore there was no yesod in the south portion of the east face (southeast corner area).
Rashi: and no yesod in the east portion of the south face (southeast again).
Rashi: meaning the southeast corner (mizrachit dromit) had no yesod.
Rashi: and Zevachim 53b explains why - Benjamin's tribe got the altar territory and no yesod belongs to him there.
Rashi: therefore there was no yesod for the southeast corner - as stated in Zevachim.
Rashi: 'since the Master said' - since Middot's yesod distribution is established, applying it here.
Rashi: in this context the northeast-to-east direction is not relevant - the concern is about which corners have a yesod.
Rashi: 'only to the right toward the east' - this general principle about rightward turns comes from Zevachim's discussion of the altar ramp.
Rashi: in Zevachim 53a the rule 'all turns are to the right' applies during the priestly service movements around the altar.
Rashi: the priest ascending the ramp turns right - so the first corner encountered is the southeast (mizrachit dromit).
Rashi: there, since there is no yesod, the priest cannot apply olah blood - he continues to the next corner.
Rashi: he circles and arrives at the northeast corner (mizrachit tzefonit) - first with a yesod present.
Rashi: there he applies the blood - even though the blood-giver stands on the floor (not the ramp), he throws from a distance as stated 've-zarku.'
Rashi: even though blood service does not happen on the ramp itself, the priest stands on the floor and throws toward the corner - below the red line (chut ha-sikra).

Rashi: even so, since for chatat blood (which goes above the red line) the priest must ascend the ramp to apply - for all blood applications the practice begins from the south face, turning rightward.
Rashi: 'perhaps for the chatat' - could the verse be referring to a New Moon chatat whose blood R. Shimon applies with olah-style rites?
Rashi: it cannot be, because the verse states 'as a chatat upon the olah of the TAMID' - limiting R. Shimon's ruling to the tamid only.
Rashi: 'we taught there in Tamid' - the mishna in Tractate Tamid about the Beit HaMoked chambers is not embedded in Yoma's Gemara because it belongs to Tamid.
Rashi: but it is cited here because the earlier passage discussed who authored the Yoma service order - and now it is R. Shimon's parallel in Tamid.
Rashi: the citation is appropriate here even though it belongs to Tamid, because of the thematic connection.
Rashi: 'who authored the Yoma service order? It is R. Shimon ish HaMitzpah' - we don't hear from him that he said this specifically.
Rashi: except because we know he disputed the Tamid service order, we attribute the Yoma anonymous text to him.
Rashi: he disagrees with the Tamid order, so the Yoma anonymous position is his.
Rashi: 'and we said in the name of Rav Huna' - Rav Huna's teaching about the Chamber of the Lambs location follows.
Rashi: the discussion raises where the Chamber of the Lambs sat in the four-corner Beit HaMoked arrangement.
Rashi: Rav Huna suggests going rightward from south: lamps, seals, Beit HaMoked, lambs.
Rashi: the counter-clockwise circuit of the four chambers starting from south.
Rashi: the circuit going south to east to north to west placing Chamber of Lambs at northwest corner.
Rashi: the challenge: if the chamber was in the northwest (as Rav Huna says from the Tamid view), what does Middot say?
Rashi: the circuit going rightward from south when the Middot author counted starting from the south - would place it differently.
Rashi: Middot lists the Beit HaMoked chambers starting from the southeast corner and going rightward.
Rashi: Middot's rightward circuit: southeast, northeast, northwest, southwest - placing Chamber of Lambs at a specific corner.
Rashi: the Shewbread chamber would end up in the northeast (mizrachit tzefonit) on this counting.
Rashi: but Middot itself says the Shewbread chamber is in the southeast - this creates the direct contradiction.
Rashi: 'we can say the Shewbread chamber is on the Shewbread' - it is in the southeast (as Middot states elsewhere), contradicting the rightward count.
Rashi: 'we taught there in Tamid' - the Tamid mishna says the Chamber of Lambs was in the northwest corner.
Rashi: the Tamid mishnah explicitly places the Chamber of Lambs in one corner, which Yoma's tradition follows.
Rashi: this citation from Tamid confirms Yoma's position about the Chamber of the Lambs location.
Rashi: 'who authored the Yoma tractate? R. Shimon' - now we revisit: Tamid places the Chamber differently from Middot.
Rashi: we do not hear R. Shimon says the Yoma service order is like this unless from the fact that he disputes Tamid's service order.
